questions.questionText: MHC Class I molecules present antigens to:
questions.explanation: MHC Class I: present endogenous (intracellular) antigens to CD8+ CTLs. Found on ALL nucleated cells (not RBCs). Encoded by HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C. Important for: viral infections, intracellular pathogens, transplant rejection, tumor immunity. MHC Class II: present exogenous (extracellular) antigens to CD4+ T helper cells. Found on APCs only (macrophages, dendritic cells, B cells). Encoded by HLA-DP, HLA-DQ, HLA-DR. Reference: Ananthanarayan — MHC.

questions.questionText: IgG is the most abundant immunoglobulin in serum and is important because it:
questions.optionB: Crosses placenta (providing passive immunity to fetus), has highest serum concentration, opsonizes bacteria, activates complement
questions.explanation: IgG: most abundant Ig in serum (~75%). ONLY Ig that crosses placenta (Fc receptor-mediated, provides passive immunity to newborn for ~6 months). Functions: opsonization (via Fc receptors on phagocytes), complement activation (classical pathway), ADCC, neutralization. 4 subclasses (IgG1-4). Secondary immune response: class-switched, high-affinity. IgM: first produced (primary response), pentamer. IgA: secretory (mucosal immunity). IgE: allergy, parasites. IgD: B cell receptor. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Immunoglobulins.

questions.optionB: BCG, oral polio (Sabin), MMR, yellow fever, varicella, rotavirus
questions.explanation: Live attenuated: weakened pathogen that replicates → strong, long-lasting immunity (both humoral + cell-mediated). Examples: BCG, oral polio (OPV/Sabin), MMR (measles, mumps, rubella), yellow fever (17D), varicella, rotavirus, nasal flu (LAIV), oral typhoid (Ty21a). Contraindicated in immunocompromised and pregnant. Killed/inactivated: Salk polio (IPV), rabies, hepatitis A, influenza (injection). Toxoid: tetanus, diphtheria. Subunit/conjugate: HBV, HPV, pneumococcal conjugate, Hib. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Vaccines.

questions.questionText: Type IV hypersensitivity (delayed-type) is mediated by:
questions.optionB: T cells (CD4+ Th1 cells and CD8+ CTLs — NOT antibody-mediated)
questions.explanation: Type IV (delayed-type hypersensitivity/DTH): T cell-mediated (NOT antibodies). Takes 48-72 hours to develop. Mechanism: sensitized T cells release cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF) → activate macrophages → tissue damage. Examples: tuberculin/Mantoux test (PPD), contact dermatitis (poison ivy, nickel), granulomatous inflammation (TB, sarcoidosis, Crohn's), transplant rejection (acute cellular), MS, Type 1 DM. No complement involvement. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Type IV Hypersensitivity.
questions.conceptTag: Type IV DTH T cell mediated

questions.optionB: Delayed-type hypersensitivity (Type IV) to tuberculin protein (PPD) — indicates prior exposure or BCG vaccination
questions.explanation: Mantoux test (tuberculin skin test/TST): intradermal injection of PPD (purified protein derivative) → read at 48-72 hours. Measures INDURATION (not erythema). ≥10mm: positive (most people). ≥5mm: positive in HIV, immunosuppressed, close contacts. Type IV (DTH) reaction mediated by memory T cells (Th1). Positive: prior exposure to M. tuberculosis OR BCG vaccination. Does NOT differentiate active from latent TB. IGRA (Interferon-Gamma Release Assay): more specific, not affected by BCG. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Mantoux Test.

questions.questionText: Complement system activation by the classical pathway is initiated by:
questions.explanation: Classical pathway: C1q binds Ag-Ab complex (IgG or IgM) → activates C1r, C1s → C4 → C2 → C3 convertase (C4b2a) → C3 → C5 → MAC (C5b-9). Alternative pathway: activated by microbial surfaces directly (no antibody needed) → C3b, Factor B, Factor D. Lectin pathway: MBL binds mannose on microbial surface → MASP → C4, C2. All converge at C3 → C5 → MAC. C3: most abundant complement component. C3b: opsonin. C5a: chemotaxis. C3a/C5a: anaphylatoxins. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Complement.
